IRISH BOOK LOVER

IRISH CHAP BOOKS.

In the eighteenth century Ireland did not possess the boon of
Education Commissioners to prepare interesting and useful school
books, nor a Kildare-place Society to issue cheap works of a harmless
and edifying character. However, as the mass of the peasantry wished
to give their children the only education they could command, namely,
that afforded by the hedge schools, and as young and old liked read-
ing stories and popular histories, or at least hearing them read, some
enterprising Dublin, Cork, and Limerick printers assumed the duties
neglected by churchmen and senators, and published ‘‘ Primers,”
“Reading-made-easys,”” °‘‘ Child’s-new-plaything,”” and the widely
diffused ‘“ Universal Spelling Book’’ of the magisterial Daniel Fen-
ning, for educational purposes. These were ‘adorned with cuts,”
but the transition from stage to stage was too abrupt, and the con-
cluding portions of the early books were as difficult as that of the
““ Universal Spelling Book *’ itself, which the author in order to render
it less practically useful, had encumbered with a dry and difficuls
grammar placed in the centre of the volume.

Two Dublin publishers, Pat Wogan, of Merchants’ Quay, and Wil-

liam Jones, 75, Thomas Street, were the educational and miscellaneous »

Alduses of the day, and considered themselves as lights burning in a

- dark place for the literary guidance of their countrymen and country-
women of the shopkeeping, farmer, and peasant classes. In the fron-
tispiece of some editions of the spelling book grew a tree of knowledge .'
laden with fruit, each marked with some letter, and ardent climbers

Plucking away. Beneath was placed this doggerel inscription:— The
tree of knowledge here you see, the fruit of which is A.B.C. But 11 you

The same followers longo intervalla of Stephens and Elzevir pub-

histories, and literary treatises, such as they were, printed with worn
type, on bad grey paper, cheaply bound in sheepskin, and sold by the
pedlars throughout the country at a tester (61d.) each. Of history,
voyages, etc., the pedlar’s pack was provided with “ Hugh Reilly’s
History of Ireland,” “ Adventures of Sir Francis Drake,” ‘° The Battle
of Aughrim,” and “Siege of Londonderry,” the two latter being
dramas, one, the composition of Robert Ashton a graduate of Trinity,
and the other generally attributed to Col. John Mitchellburne, a de-
fender of Derry; ““ Life and Adventures of James Freney the Robber,”
“The Irish Rogues and Rapparees,” ‘““The Trojan Wars,” ‘“ Troy’s
Destruction,” ‘“ The Life of Baron Trenck,” and ‘“ The Nine Worthies—
Three Jews, Three Heathens, and Three Christians.”” Every reader is
familiar with Thackeray’s description in the ¢ Irish Sketch Book,”
of the pleasure he derived from the perusal of some of these when

The list would not be complete without mention of the garlands
and books of ballads. These were printed in Waterford, Monaghan,
and other provincial towns, and sold in sheets, each forming 8 pages,
18mo., and adorned with cuts, seldom germane to the subject. Some of
these sheets contained only one production; as the ‘ Yarmouth
Tragedy,” or some other early English ballad, others contained a
selection.

The purchasers of these sheets sewed them as well as they could
in book form, but they were so thumbed and used, that it is at this
date nearly impossible to procure one of these repetoires of song,
printed towards the close of the 18Th or beginning of the 19th century.
The British Museum fortunately possesses a large collection of them.

The following, up till forty years ago, tormed portion of the ped-
lar’s stock:—“ The Academy of Compliments,” ‘° The Arabian Nights,”
*“ The Battle of Aughrim,” *“ Zsop,” “ Gulliver,”” ‘ O’Reilly’s Ireland,”
“The Cottagers of Glenburnie,” ‘ Hocus Pocus,” “ Irish Rogues,”
“James I'reney,” ‘“Robin Hood’s Garland,” ‘Seven Champions,”
"“Tales of the Fairies,” ‘“ The Trojan Wars,” ‘ Valentine and Orson,”’
““The Seven Wise Masters and Mistresses of Rome,” some of them

absolutely harmless, whilst forty years before Joseph Smyth, of Bel-

fast, issued a catalogue of no less than 70 similar works. el

and I desire to draw attention to them in the I.B.L., as I fear that
they have not received the extensive attention which they deserve.
The writer is Mr. R. Burch and all Irish bibliographers owe him a debt
of gratitude for the greabt trouble and pains he has taken, and the
systematic and thorough way he has dealt with the history of paper-
making in Ireland. His first article on Irish Paper-making history
appeared in the issue of the above-mentioned journal of 23rd J uly, 1909.
The writer, (who .is connected with the firm of W. J. Stonhill and Co.,
of Shoe Lane, London), has gone into the matter with a thoroughness
whiph the subject has never before received. In fact so far as I know,
beyond casual allusions in some journals to the subject, it has never
before been dealt with at all. Mr. Burch in his opening article states
that he cannot find any reference to Paper-making in Ireland earlier
than the year 1590, and that practically very lkittle is on record at all
of the manufacture of paper in Ireland until the last decade of the 17th
century, when some of the French refugees obtained patents for mak-
ing writing and printing paper. It would not do justice to the writer
or o the subject to quote from his work. I can only hope that when
he has completed the series, for it is not yet complete, his articles will
be published in pamphlet or book form. He has made researches not
only in the old Patent Rolls in England, but here as well, and has
gleaned an immense amount of information from King’s letters,
patents, etc., and he has carried his research down, so far, into the
‘middle of the 18th century. His second article appears in the journal
already mentioned bearing date the 26th November, 1909. The first of
‘the two articles dealt almost entirely with the work done here, mills
~ get up and so forth, by these French refugees and others in the end
- of ithe 17th century. He gives, later on, returns from papers of the

JOURNALS AND JOURNALISM IN DERRY.

The note by Mr. Dix in the March number, on the discovery of the
Derry Journals, in Magee College Library, prompts me to write that
at the break-up of the City newsroom about 1895, the bound volumes
of all the Derry papers—George Douglas’s present amongst them, were
““ bought for a song,” by the late Mr. Moore, of Mollenan, Derry, and
are probably in the possession of his successors there. The first news-
paper published in Derry—it may interest your readers to know—made
its appearance in June, 1772, its full title being ‘“ The Londonderry
Journal and Donegal and Tyrone Advertiser.” It was owned and
edited by George Douglas, a Scotsman, who in April, 1796, sold it to
John Buchanan and William McCorkell, and left for America in the
following Augast. It appeared weekly until 1863, then bi-weekly till

~ 1872. Since when it has been tri-weekly. It was at first an indepen-

dent organ, put became Tory, and so continued until 1829, when it

was purchased by the Whigs. It retained this policy until 1881, when
it became and remains Nationalist. In its columns appeared many of
